perturbation.original_text: Section 4.1. Term. The term of this Agreement commences on the Effective Date and continues in perpetuity, unless termination occurs pursuant to Sections 4.2 through 4.4.
perturbation.changed_text: Section 4.1. Term. The term of this Agreement commences on the Effective Date and continues for a period of five (5) years, unless termination occurs pursuant to Sections 4.2 through 4.4.
perturbation.explanation: This change creates a contradiction regarding the agreement's duration. Initially, it was set for 'in perpetuity,' but now it's limited to 'five (5) years.' This directly contradicts the 'in perpetuity' clause, causing uncertainty about the actual term and enforcement of termination conditions after the 5 year period.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Section 4.1. Term. The term of this Agreement commences on the Effective Date and continues for a period of five (5) years, unless termination occurs pursuant to Sections 4.2 through 4.4.
perturbation.justification: The original text stated the agreement continues 'in perpetuity,' meaning forever. The changed text limits the agreement to 'five (5) years.' The contradicted text, which is the same as the changed text, states the agreement is for 'five (5) years'. Therefore, the change from 'in perpetuity' to 'five (5) years' directly contradicts the original perpetual term, creating an inconsistency that would impact the enforceability and interpretation of the contract's duration, directly conflicting with the contradicted section.

perturbation.original_text: Section 4.2. Termination for Convenience. Licensor reserves the right to terminate this Agreement immediately upon written notice for any reason, including if the usage of the Brand is not in compliance with the standards and policies.
perturbation.changed_text: Section 4.2. Termination for Convenience. Licensor reserves the right to terminate this Agreement immediately upon written notice for any reason, including if the usage of the Brand is not in compliance with the standards and policies. However, Licensor can only exercise this right within the first two years of the agreement.
perturbation.explanation: By adding 'However, Licensor can only exercise this right within the first two years of the agreement', we are limiting the Licensor's right to terminate for convenience. This creates a contradiction because the initial statement allows for immediate termination at any time for any reason, while the added clause restricts this right to a two-year period. This affects section 4.2 since we are modifying the original definition of the Terminating for Convenience, the added limitation creates a contradiction within the text.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Section 4.2. Termination for Convenience. Licensor reserves the right to terminate this Agreement immediately upon written notice for any reason, including if the usage of the Brand is not in compliance with the standards and policies. However, Licensor can only exercise this right within the first two years of the agreement.
perturbation.justification: The changed text adds a time limitation to the Licensor's right to terminate the agreement for convenience ('However, Licensor can only exercise this right within the first two years of the agreement'), whereas the original text and contradicted text state that the Licensor can terminate the agreement 'immediately upon written notice for any reason'. This creates a direct conflict because the modified clause restricts a previously unrestricted right, creating an inconsistency regarding when the Licensor can terminate for convenience, thus contradicting the original statement.

perturbation.original_text: Section 9.6. No Waiver; Cumulative Remedies. No failure or delay by a party to exercise any right hereunder, in whole or in part, shall operate as a waiver thereof. The parties' rights and remedies herein are cumulative and not exclusive of any other rights and remedies provided by applicable Law.
perturbation.changed_text: Section 9.6. No Waiver; Cumulative Remedies. No failure or delay by a party to exercise any right hereunder, in whole or in part, shall operate as a waiver thereof, unless such delay exceeds six months. The parties' rights and remedies herein are cumulative and not exclusive of any other rights and remedies provided by applicable Law.
perturbation.explanation: The original clause states 'No failure or delay by a party to exercise any right hereunder, in whole or in part, shall operate as a waiver thereof,' which means a party doesn't lose their rights even if they delay exercising them. The added condition 'unless such delay exceeds six months' directly contradicts this by introducing a time limit after which a delay does constitute a waiver. This in-text contradiction creates confusion and uncertainty about when a party's rights are actually waived, potentially leading to disputes.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Section 9.6. No Waiver; Cumulative Remedies. No failure or delay by a party to exercise any right hereunder, in whole or in part, shall operate as a waiver thereof, unless such delay exceeds six months. The parties' rights and remedies herein are cumulative and not exclusive of any other rights and remedies provided by applicable Law.
perturbation.justification: The original text stated that no delay would constitute a waiver. The changed text introduces an exception: delays exceeding six months do constitute a waiver. This directly contradicts the initial absolute statement and creates an inconsistency as described in the explanation. Since the Changed Text is identical to the Contradicted Text, it means that the Change is contradicting the Contradicted Text and there is an in-text contradiction.
